summary: McKenzie outlines his plan to localize Bingo Card Creator for the Japanese market, where he lives. He built the software with internationalization from the start, making the technical work trivial. The real opportunity is an unserved market of English teachers in Japan who need classroom materials. The main challenges are localizing the marketing website and finding payment processors that support Japanese payment methods, not the software itself.
key_points: Building software with localization support from day one makes internationalization nearly trivial later
The Japanese market for English teaching materials is completely unserved, representing a greenfield opportunity
Marketing localization is far harder than product localization — writing persuasive copy in another language is a different skill than translating UI strings
Payment processing in Japan requires supporting alternative payment methods beyond credit cards
Advertising costs for niche keywords in non-English markets can be dramatically lower due to less competition
money_quotes: My market is underserved in the US, but it just isn't served at all in Japan.
Think of it: how many people are bidding on English teaching keywords in Japan? Answer: not really that many, except folks recruiting for jobs.
I'm a lot less confident in my ability to market in Japanese than in my ability to write a Save As dialog in Japanese.
Going by the results of my marketing in English, I've not exactly got natural talent for it.
Credit card penetration is not quite 100% here (there are a witches brew of microcurrencies and alternative payment methods).
key_insight: The hard part of entering a foreign market isn't translating the product — it's translating the marketing and adapting to local payment infrastructure.
